content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 12, 1997
Honorable Rick Bennetts
City of Mount Shasta
204 N. Washington Drive
Mount Shasta, California 96067
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-280
Dear Mayor Bennetts:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
May you participate in a governmental decision regarding a new bottled water facility to
be built immediately adjacent to rental property you own?
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in any governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on your rental property.
FACTS
You and your wife own rental property located within the Mt. Shasta City limits. It is
zoned commercial but currently contains two residential units. The property is located at 131 Ski
Village Drive and is about 0.4 acres in size. The real property line is congruous with the Mt.
Shasta City boundary line.
Immediately adjacent to your property, outside the city limits in Siskiyou County, is a
former lumber mill property. It is presently being studied by an international corporation as a
possible site to locate a large bottled water facility. If the company purchases this property, they
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-280
Page 2
would have to obtain all permits and all environmental clearances through Siskiyou County.
However, the company would be negotiating with the City ofMt. Shasta for the following
services and improvements to city property: 1) sale of city water for bottling purposes; 2) city
sewer and water utility extensions, upgrades, and connections; 3) the possible upgrading of Ski
Village Drive within city limits for safety reasons to handle additional truck traffic; and,
4) other services as needed.
You and your wife have owned this rental property since 1987. You and your wife have
never lived at this location and anticipate no change in the use ofthis property in the foreseeable
future. You do not anticipate any change in the value ofyour property if the adjacent former mill
property is utilized as a bottled water plant. The actual manufacturing plant would not be located
adjacent to your property but would be located on the opposite side of Ski Village Drive.
ANALYSIS
Introduction
The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose ofthe conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that public officials would
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Tn furtherance of
this goal, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
You are a public official according to the Act. (Regulation 18700.) Regulation 18700
also determines when a public official makes a decision or participates in making a decision.
Regulation 18700, in pertinent part, provides:
“(b) A public official ‘makes a governmental decision,’ except as
provided in subdivision (d) ofthis section, when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position:
(1) Votes on a matter;
(2) Appoints a person;
(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;
(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf ofhis or her
agency;
(5) Determines not to act, within the meaning of subdivisions
(b)(1), (b)(2), (b)(3), or (b)(4) above, unless such determination is
made because of his or her financial interest. When the
determination not to act occurs because of the official’s financial
interest, the official’s determination must be accompanied by
disclosure of the financial interest, made part ofthe agency’s


File No. A-97-280
Page 3
official record, or made in writing to the official’s supervisor as
provided in 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18730(b)(l0), to the
appointing power, or to any other person specified in a Conflict of
Interest Code adopted pursuant to Government Code Section
87300.
(c) A public official “participates in making a governmental
decision,” except as provided in subdivision (d) of this regulation,
when, acting within the authority ofhis or her position, the official:
(I) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a
governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental
decision referenced in subdivision (a)(2)(A) above;
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
either directly or without significant intervening substantive
review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part ofthe official and the
purpose ofwhich is to influence a governmental decision
referenced in subdivision (a)(2)(A) above; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on
the part of the official and the purpose ofwhich is to influence a
governmental decision referenced in subdivision (a)(2)(A) above.
(d) Making or participating in making a governmental decision
shall not include:
(1) Actions ofpublic officials which are solely ministerial,
secretarial, manual, or clerical;
(2) Appearances by a public official as a member ofthe general
public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters
related solely to the official’s personal interests as defined in 2 Cal.
Code ofRegs. Section 18700.1(b)(l); or
(3) Actions by public officials relating to their compensation or
the terms or conditions oftheir employment or contract. In the
case ofpublic officials who are “consultants,” as defined above,
this includes actions by consultants relating to the terms or
conditions of the contract pursuant to which they provide services
to the agency, so long as they are acting in their private capacity.”
You have not provided specific facts on how exactly you will be involved in decisions
regarding the bottled water plant. Please use the above regulation to determine whether you are
making a governmental decision, or participating in making a governmental decision. We have
also enclosed a copy of Regulation 18700.1 which will help you determine when you are
influencing a governmental decision.


File No. A-97-280
Page 4
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member ofhis or her immediate
family or on, among other things: 1) any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more; 2) any source of income
aggregating two hundred and fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
2
You have stated that you have an economic interest in real property. Assuming this
interest is worth more than $1,000, you have an economic interest in the rental property located
on Ski Village Drive. You must disqualify yourself from any governmental decision at issue if
the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in real
property, distinguishable from the public generally.
You also have stated that you currently rent the property at issue. The tenants ofyour
rental unit are a source of income to you if you receive $250 or more per year from them.
Assuming that you do, you must disqualify yourself from any governmental decision at issue if
the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your source of
income, distinguishable from the public generally.
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) We must apply a foreseeability
analysis to each economic interest. First, your interest in real property.
You state in your letter that you do not believe that your property value will be affected
by the new business being built immediately adjacent to your property. To support your
conclusion you state that the actual manufacturing plant will be located on the opposite side of
your rental property. However, you also state that ifthe bottled water business goes forward
many improvements to city property will be necessary. For example, there will be city sewer and
water upgrades and Ski Village Drive, the street on which you live, will be upgraded to handle
additional traffic. You have not explained why these improvements and the additional truck
traffic will not affect your real property value. Considering the latter and the fact that the
2 Section 87103 also includes other economic interests which do not seem to be implicated by your facts.
(Section 87 103(a), (d) and (e).)


File No. A-97-280
PageS
property where the proposed facility may be built is currently vacant, we conclude that the
addition of a manufacturing business on real property immediately adjacent to your real property
will likely change the property values, up or down, surrounding the proposed site and therefore
meets the foreseeability standard.
Next, we must apply a foreseeability analysis to your source of income, your tenants.
Again, an effect on a source of income is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. We do not have sufficient facts to make a final
determination of foreseeability as to this economic interest.
Materiality
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is still only required where the foreseeable
effect on the public official’s economic interest is material. The Commission has adopted a
series of regulations that provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects
of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702.) The standard for materiality differs depending
on the type of economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in a decision.
3 Since you have not provided specific information about any
particular governmental decision, we cannot conclusively determine whether any of your
economic interests are directly involved. Most likely, your economic interest will be indirectly
affected.
Regulation 18702.3 governs an ownership interest in real property indirectly involved in
a governmental decision (copy enclosed). Regulation 18702.3, in pertinent part, states:
“(a) The effect of a decision is material as to real property in
which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial ownership
interest (not including a leasehold interest), if any of the following
applies:
(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part ofthat real property, is located within a 300 foot radius ofthe
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject ofthe decision, unless the decision will have no
financial effect upon the official’s real property interest.
“A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an
official’s agency when that person is directly involved in a decision before an
official’s agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official’s agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity.”


File No. A-97-280
Page 6
(2) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new
or substantially improved services.
(3) The real property in which the official has an interest is
located outside a radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of
the decision and the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on the fair market
value ofthe real property in which the official has an interest; or
(B) Will affect the rental value ofthe property by $1,000 or more
per 12 month period.”
You have not provided sufficient facts to determine the issue ofmateriality. You have
indicated that your property is adjacent to boundary and that some decisions may involve
improvements. If any subdivision of the above regulation applies, you may not participate in the
governmental decision. For example, ifthe boundary of your property and the former mill
property is within 300 feet, you may not participate in a decision unless you can demonstrate that
there will be no effect on your real property. (Barisone Advice Letter, A-95-082.) You must
apply the above regulation to your situation to determine whether or not any financial effect is
material.
Assuming foreseeability, a financial effect is material on a source of income who is an
individual
4 indirectly involved in the decision when:
The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a
source of income or gifts to an official if any of the following
applies:
(a) The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual’s real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or
18702.4.”
You must determine whether this materiality standard is met. If any subdivision of the above
regulation applies, you may not participate in the governmental decision.
Public Generally
If your tenant is a business, you must apply Regulation 18702.2 instead (copy enclosed).


File No. A-97-280
Page 7
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is material,
disqualification is required only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. (Section 87103.) For the “public generally” exception to apply, a decision must affect
the official’s interest in substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of
the public. Regulation 18703 sets out two types oftests to determine what constitutes a
significant segment ofthe public (copy enclosed).
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
4-fl
By:
Marte Castaños
StaffCounsel, Legal Division
SGC:MC:jlw

May you participate in a governmental decision regarding a new bottled water facility to

foreseeable and material financial effect on your rental property.

entity.”
(2) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the

or substantially improved services.

applies:

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you participate in a governmental decision regarding a new bottled water facility to
be built immediately adjacent to rental property you own?

CONCLUSION: You may not participate in any governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on your rental property.
